It’s May Day, international communist day. It’s also a Satanic unholy day, which is not a coincidence. There is much evidence Karl Marx was a dark occultist. 

https://t.co/T3yV4jlcuC

What should happen on this day is that we as Americans should be celebrating that we have NOT fully succumbed to communism on our shores.

We’ve avoided the mass death and genocide.

And we still have an economy with important strands of capitalism, especially at the small-business level.

But what do we actually have?

A largely complacent populace coupled with these rabble-rousing idiots demanding that communism come and take away their freedoms, and eventually their lives.

kept.text:
Here’s why I love e-commerce businesses as investments. $SE $MELI $AMZN

When you become the dominant e-commerce player in a market, you develop a customer relationship with virtually every single person in that market. How many Americans do you know that have never used Amazon?

This gives you a massive distribution network to enter new, higher margin verticals. Payments and broader fintech, advertising, streaming, gaming, food delivery, live entertainment, travel, etc.

Every time a user checks out is an opportunity to cross sell them your other services. And when most of them have used your platform several times over many years, trust has already been built. 

E-commerce itself can be a tough business with low margins, high competition, and lots of reinvestment required. But it’s also an incredible advertisement, essentially a giant billboard across an entire region.

kept.text:
Top of the morning ☕📈

Andy Jassy $AMZN
“Every single application, every single business is going to be reinvented with AI.”

This isn’t incremental. AWS is the backbone of the AI build out. Alexa is being rebuilt. Logistics, retail, advertising are all getting rewired from the inside out.

Lesson: own the infrastructure, not just the apps. Amazon isn’t chasing AI trends, they’re selling the picks and shovels. The boring back-end is where generational wealth gets built.

Stay bullish 🤝

kept.text: $AMZN CEO: "AI is commonly seen as a GPU story, but the rise of agentic workloads, real-time reasoning, code generation, reinforcement learning, and multi-step task orchestration is driving massive CPU demand as well. As AI systems shift from answering questions to taking actions, and as post-training and inference scale up, the compute required pulls heavily on CPU"

profile_bio.description: We write a weekly newsletter of quality quotes from Earnings Calls | Editor: @Skrisiloff | Lead Author: @ekmokaya | Publisher: @Avondaleam |

quoted_tweet.text: $INTC Intel CEO last week: "I think the feedback from the customer, CPU is very important when you move from training to inference. Inference side, I think in terms of orchestration, control plane and also managing all the different agent with data, CPU is much more efficient. So I think the ratio of CPU to GPU used to be 1 and 8, and now it's 1:4 and I think towards parity or even better. So I think the demand is very strong.

kept.text:
AWS up 28% and the market's sleeping on $AMZN's chip business? That's the real alpha here.

Q1 2026 was a breakout quarter — AWS carrying the load with 28% revenue growth and sequential improvements across every segment. But the Trainium/Inferentia chip play is flying under the radar as a legit $NVDA challenger for internal AI workloads.

This is a multi-catalyst story. Cloud reacceleration + proprietary silicon = margin expansion nobody's pricing in. Watching $AMZN for continuation.

#Amazon #AWS #TechStocks

We are still early.

tweet.text:
Big Tech CapEx has reached unprecedented levels:

The combined CapEx of Amazon, $AMZN, Google, $GOOG, Meta, $META, and Microsoft, $MSFT, is expected to surge +98% YoY, to a record $715 billion in 2026.

This is nearly 3 TIMES the amount spent in 2024 and more than 5 TIMES 2023 levels.

Amazon now expects CapEx to approach a record $200 billion this year.

This is followed by Google and Microsoft which are both guiding $190 billion in CapEx for 2026.

Meta has also raised its expectations by +$10 billion to $125-$145 billion.

To put this differently, each company is projected to spend nearly as much in 2026 as they did in the previous 2 years combined, or more.

The AI Revolution is set to accelerate.

$SPY $TSLA $AMZN $GOOGL $QQQ $HOOD $NVDA $AAPL $MFST $AMD $META $PLTR $CAR $BYND $OPEN $RKLB $ASTS $MVRL $QCOM https://t.co/vE0K43Xun9

quoted_tweet.text:
Good morning ☕️🦀🇺🇸 Happy Saturday!

The stock market had a strong week overall for the period ending Friday, May 2, 2026 (trading days April 28–May 1), with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq posting weekly gains and new record closes, while the Dow was slightly negative.

Major Indices Performance (Week of April 28–May 1)
•S&P 500: Closed at 7,230.12 (+0.29% on Friday; weekly gain ~0.3–0.6% range across reports). It hit new all-time highs multiple times.
•Nasdaq Composite: Closed at 25,114.44 (+0.89% on Friday; stronger weekly gain). Also set new records, driven by tech.
•Dow Jones Industrial Average: Closed at 49,499.27 (-0.31% on Friday; slight weekly loss).

April as a whole was exceptional: The S&P 500 gained over 9–10% (best monthly performance since 2020), Nasdaq ~15% (best since 2020), and Dow ~7%. This reflected a sharp recovery from earlier geopolitical pressures.

Key Drivers and Themes
•Strong Corporate Earnings: A heavy earnings week boosted sentiment. Big Tech results were mixed but generally supportive:
◦Apple (AAPL) reported strong Q2 results on April 30: Revenue $111.2B (+17% YoY), EPS $2.01 (+22% YoY), beating estimates. Shares rose >3% on Friday.
◦Other highlights included Alphabet (strong results), Caterpillar (big Dow boost), and mixed Big Tech reactions around AI capex/spending concerns (e.g., Meta, Microsoft).
•Geopolitics and Oil: Ongoing U.S.-Iran tensions and Strait of Hormuz disruptions kept oil volatile and elevated (WTI near $100+/barrel at points, with spikes higher). This pressured some sectors but markets largely shrugged it off amid ceasefire hopes and resilient earnings.
•Breadth and Style: Tech and growth led (especially AI-related), while broader market participation was narrower. Value and small-caps were more mixed.
•Economic Backdrop: Resilient U.S. data (e.g., retail sales, PMIs) supported the rally despite Fed signals and inflation concerns. Markets showed optimism heading into May.

Sector and Market Notes
•Tech, communication services, and select cyclicals (e.g., industrials) outperformed.
•Energy was volatile with oil prices.
•Defensive areas lagged somewhat.
•International markets were mixed, with some developed markets underperforming U.S. indices.

Overall Sentiment: Bullish recovery mode. The market demonstrated resilience amid geopolitical noise, rewarding earnings strength and AI optimism. “Sell in May” concerns were discussed but not dominating, given recent momentum.

This recap is based on closing levels and reports as of May 1–2, 2026. Markets move fast—always check real-time sources for the latest.
